title: 9.8 Nutrient Absorption
expanded_description: An introduction to how the body breaks down food into molecules small enough to absorb, and how those molecules cross the intestinal wall into the bloodstream or lymphatic system. This guide explains the chemical digestion of carbohydrates, proteins, and fats by specific enzymes; the structure of the small intestine that makes absorption efficient; and the different pathways for sugars, amino acids, and fats to enter the body. It also covers the absorption of water, vitamins, and minerals, and what happens when absorption fails.

core_concept.principle: Nutrients must be broken down into molecules small enough to cross the intestinal lining. Carbohydrates are digested to monosaccharides (glucose, fructose, galactose); proteins to amino acids; fats to fatty acids and monoglycerides. These products are absorbed through the small intestine wall—sugars and amino acids enter blood vessels (capillaries), while fats enter lymph vessels (lacteals). Vitamins, minerals, and water are absorbed by various mechanisms, some requiring special transport proteins.
core_concept.explanation:
Your body can't absorb a whole sandwich. It needs to break down large food molecules into smaller building blocks that can pass through the cells lining your small intestine. This breakdown happens through chemical digestion—enzymes cutting large molecules into smaller ones.

The three main nutrient types and their end products:
• Carbohydrates (starches, sugars) → broken down into monosaccharides: glucose, fructose, galactose.
• Proteins → broken down into amino acids (and some small peptides).
• Fats (lipids) → broken down into fatty acids and monoglycerides.

Where digestion happens:
• Mouth: Salivary amylase starts breaking starch.
• Stomach: Pepsin starts breaking protein.
• Small intestine: Most chemical digestion happens here. Pancreatic enzymes (amylase, trypsin, lipase) and brush border enzymes (on the surface of intestinal cells) complete the breakdown.

The absorption surface:
The small intestine is lined with finger-like projections called villi (singular: villus). Each villus contains:
• Blood capillaries: Absorb monosaccharides and amino acids.
• Lacteal: A tiny lymph vessel that absorbs fatty acids and monoglycerides (repackaged into chylomicrons).

The surface of each villus cell has even smaller projections called microvilli (brush border). Together, folds, villi, and microvilli give the small intestine a surface area of about 300 square meters—the size of a tennis court!

Special absorption facts:
• Vitamin B12 requires "intrinsic factor" from the stomach to be absorbed.
• Iron absorption is enhanced by vitamin C and reduced by tannins (tea) and calcium.
• Calcium absorption requires vitamin D.
• Water is absorbed mostly in the small intestine, but the large intestine recovers the final 10-15% to form solid stool.

Fun fact: The cells lining your small intestine are replaced every 3-5 days. They take a beating from enzymes, acid, and the constant movement of food—so your body constantly makes new ones!

image.alt: A diagram of a villus in the small intestine showing: blood capillaries (absorb glucose and amino acids), lacteal (absorbs fats), and epithelial cells with microvilli (brush border). Three panels show: carbohydrates → glucose into blood; proteins → amino acids into blood; fats → fatty acids into lymph.
image.caption: Nutrient absorption in the small intestine: Sugars and amino acids enter blood vessels; fats enter lymph vessels (lacteals). The villus structure creates enormous surface area for absorption.

sections.introduction:
You eat food, but your cells don't eat sandwiches. They need simple molecules: glucose for energy, amino acids to build proteins, fatty acids for membranes and energy storage. The process of turning a meal into these building blocks is called digestion and absorption.

The journey of a nutrient:
1. Ingestion: You eat.
2. Mechanical digestion: Chewing, churning—breaks food into smaller pieces, increases surface area.
3. Chemical digestion: Enzymes break chemical bonds, turning large molecules into smaller ones.
4. Absorption: Small molecules cross the intestinal wall into blood or lymph.
5. Transport: Nutrients travel to the liver (via portal vein) for processing, then to the rest of the body.

Your small intestine is the star of absorption. It's about 20 feet long, but its surface area is huge because of:
• Circular folds: Spiral ridges that slow food movement and increase surface area.
• Villi: Finger-like projections (1 mm tall) covering the folds.
• Microvilli: Even smaller projections on each villus cell (brush border).

Each villus contains a network of blood capillaries and a central lymph vessel (lacteal). Different nutrients take different paths after absorption:
• Glucose and amino acids: Enter blood → go to liver → then to the rest of the body.
• Fats: Enter lymph → eventually enter blood near the heart (bypassing the liver initially).

Your body is incredibly efficient at absorption. Under normal conditions, about 95% of the nutrients you eat are absorbed. The remaining 5% (mostly fiber) becomes part of your stool.

When absorption fails—due to disease (celiac disease, Crohn's), surgery, or infections—you can become malnourished even if you're eating enough. This is called malabsorption syndrome.

introduction_image.alt: A diagram showing layers of the small intestine wall: circular folds (large ridges), villi (finger-like projections), and microvilli (tiny hairs). A comparison of surface area: smooth tube (small) vs. with folds (larger) vs. with villi (much larger) vs. with microvilli (largest).
introduction_image.caption: The small intestine's surface area is huge because of circular folds, villi, and microvilli. This allows efficient absorption of nutrients.

key_concept.definition: Carbohydrates are digested to monosaccharides, proteins to amino acids, and fats to fatty acids and monoglycerides. These end products are absorbed in the small intestine: sugars and amino acids enter blood; fats enter lymph. Water, vitamins, and minerals are absorbed by specialized mechanisms.
key_concept.context:
This division of labor makes sense: water-soluble nutrients (sugars, amino acids) need to go directly to the liver for processing; fats (which don't mix well with blood) take a detour through the lymphatic system, entering the bloodstream near the heart.

The speed of absorption varies:
• Glucose: Absorbed quickly (15-30 minutes).
• Amino acids: Absorbed within 1-2 hours.
• Fats: Slowest, taking 3-6 hours because they need to be emulsified and processed.

Different nutrients require different transporters:
• Glucose and galactose: Use active transport (SGLT1)—requires energy and sodium.
• Fructose: Uses facilitated diffusion (GLUT5)—passive, no energy.
• Amino acids: Several different active transport systems (for neutral, acidic, basic amino acids).
• Fatty acids: Diffuse into cells (passive) then are repackaged.

Real-world connection: Sports drinks often contain both glucose and fructose. Using two different transporters allows faster absorption of carbohydrates during exercise. Too much of one type (like pure glucose) can overwhelm that transporter and cause stomach upset.

image.alt: A villus with blood capillaries (red) and lacteal (white/yellow). Glucose and amino acids shown entering blood vessels. Fatty acids shown entering the lacteal. A small icon of the liver and heart showing the different paths.
image.caption: Absorption pathways: Sugars and amino acids enter blood and go to the liver. Fats enter lymph and eventually reach the bloodstream near the heart.

sections.key_concept: Chemical digestion uses specific enzymes to break down macromolecules. Absorption uses specialized transporters and structures to move nutrients across the intestinal barrier. Different nutrients follow different routes into the body.

classifications.value: Understanding how starches and sugars become glucose, and how glucose enters the blood.
characteristics: Sources: Starches (bread, pasta, rice), disaccharides (sucrose—table sugar, lactose—milk sugar), monosaccharides (glucose, fructose).

Enzymes: Salivary amylase (mouth) and pancreatic amylase (small intestine) break starch into disaccharides (maltose, isomaltose). Brush border enzymes (maltase, sucrase, lactase) break disaccharides into monosaccharides.
Lactose intolerance: Lack of lactase means lactose isn't broken down. Undigested lactose reaches large intestine, where bacteria ferment it → gas, bloating, diarrhea.

Absorption: Glucose and galactose absorbed via SGLT1 (sodium-glucose co-transporter)—active transport (requires energy, sodium). Fructose absorbed via GLUT5—facilitated diffusion (passive). All three exit intestinal cells into blood via GLUT2.
Fate: Blood carries glucose to liver. Liver can store as glycogen, release to blood, or convert to fat.
classifications.behavior:
Carbohydrate digestion starts in the mouth, pauses in the stomach (acid inactivates amylase), and finishes in the small intestine. Pancreatic amylase is more powerful than salivary amylase and does most of the work.

The SGLT1 transporter is fascinating: it uses the sodium gradient (sodium higher outside cells, lower inside) to pull glucose in against its concentration gradient. This is why oral rehydration solutions (for diarrhea) contain both salt (sodium) and sugar—the sugar helps pull sodium into the body, and the sodium helps pull water. This saves lives in cholera and other diarrheal diseases.

Fructose absorption is slower and capacity-limited. Consuming large amounts of fructose (from high-fructose corn syrup, fruit juice) can overwhelm GLUT5, leading to fructose malabsorption—gas, bloating, diarrhea. This is different from lactose intolerance (enzyme deficiency), but symptoms are similar.

Clinical connection: In diabetes mellitus, glucose absorption is normal, but cells can't take it up properly (lack of insulin or insulin resistance). Blood glucose rises. The kidneys try to excrete the excess, leading to glucose in urine (glycosuria) and increased urination (polyuria).

examples.process: Oral Rehydration Therapy: Saving Lives with Sugar and Salt
examples.explanation:
Oral rehydration solution (ORS) is a simple mixture of water, sugar (glucose), and salt (sodium chloride). It has saved millions of lives from cholera and other diarrheal diseases: (1) Problem: Severe diarrhea causes loss of water and electrolytes. Dehydration can kill, especially in children. (2) Mechanism: The SGLT1 transporter in the small intestine co-transports glucose and sodium. When both are present, water follows osmotically. (3) ORS works even during diarrhea: The transporters continue to work, pulling water into the body despite the diarrhea. (4) Why plain water isn't enough: Without the sodium-glucose co-transport, water absorption is reduced. Also, plain water dilutes blood sodium, which can be dangerous. (5) Recipe: WHO ORS contains 2.6 g sodium chloride, 2.9 g trisodium citrate, 1.5 g potassium chloride, and 13.5 g glucose per liter of water.

Why this matters: Understanding the mechanism of glucose and sodium co-transport led to a simple, cheap treatment that has reduced childhood deaths from diarrhea by millions.

image.alt: A diagram showing the SGLT1 transporter: sodium and glucose entering the cell together, water following. A child drinking ORS from a cup. A packet of ORS with ingredients. A graph showing mortality from diarrhea dropping after ORS introduction.
image.caption: Oral rehydration solution (ORS) uses the SGLT1 transporter to pull water into the body during diarrhea, saving millions of lives.

classifications.value: Understanding how proteins become amino acids, and how amino acids enter the blood.
characteristics: Sources: Meat, fish, eggs, dairy, beans, nuts, grains.

Enzymes: Pepsin (stomach) breaks proteins into large peptides. Pancreatic proteases (trypsin, chymotrypsin) break peptides into smaller peptides. Brush border peptidases break small peptides into amino acids (and some dipeptides/tripeptides).
Pepsin activation: Stomach acid (HCl) converts inactive pepsinogen to active pepsin. Pepsin then activates more pepsinogen (positive feedback).

Absorption: Amino acids are absorbed via several active transport systems (specific for neutral, acidic, basic amino acids). Dipeptides and tripeptides are absorbed via PEPT1 transporter, then broken down inside cells. All exit into blood.
Fate: Amino acids travel to liver. Used to build new proteins, make enzymes, or (if excess) converted to glucose or fat.
classifications.behavior:
Protein digestion is unique because it starts in the stomach. The low pH (1.5-3.5) denatures proteins (unfolds them), making them easier for enzymes to cut. Pepsin specifically cuts after certain amino acids (tyrosine, phenylalanine).

Trypsin and chymotrypsin (from pancreas) are secreted as inactive precursors (trypsinogen, chymotrypsinogen). This is protective—if they activated in the pancreas, they'd digest the pancreas itself! In pancreatitis, this is exactly what happens: enzymes activate prematurely, causing the pancreas to digest itself.

Amino acid absorption is efficient—over 99% of protein is absorbed under normal conditions. The transporters are specific: there's one for neutral amino acids, one for acidic, one for basic, etc. Genetic defects in these transporters can cause diseases like cystinuria (inability to absorb cystine, leading to kidney stones).

Unlike glucose, there's no "protein" in blood—only amino acids. After a protein-rich meal, amino acid levels in blood rise, stimulating insulin release (which helps cells take up amino acids).

Clinical connection: In cystic fibrosis, the pancreas is damaged by thick secretions, leading to pancreatic insufficiency. Without enough pancreatic enzymes, proteins aren't properly digested. Undigested protein in the stool causes foul-smelling, greasy stools (steatorrhea). Treatment: pancreatic enzyme replacement therapy (PERT)—taking enzyme capsules with meals.

examples.explanation:
Cystic fibrosis (CF) is a genetic disorder that affects the CFTR chloride channel. In the pancreas, thick mucus blocks the ducts, preventing digestive enzymes from reaching the small intestine: (1) What happens: The pancreas produces enzymes, but they can't exit. The pancreas becomes scarred (fibrotic). Without enzymes reaching the intestine, proteins and fats aren't digested. (2) Symptoms: Failure to thrive (can't absorb nutrients), steatorrhea (fatty, foul-smelling stools), vitamin deficiencies (especially fat-soluble vitamins A, D, E, K). (3) Diagnosis: Fecal elastase (low indicates pancreatic insufficiency). (4) Treatment: Pancreatic enzyme replacement therapy (PERT)—capsules containing lipase, protease, amylase are taken with every meal and snack. The dose is adjusted based on meal size and fat content. (5) Outcome: With proper enzyme replacement, people with CF can absorb nutrients normally, though they still face other challenges (lung disease, etc.).

Why this matters: CF shows that even if digestion is normal in the gut, you need the enzymes from the pancreas. Without them, malabsorption occurs despite eating enough.

image.alt: A diagram of the pancreas showing blocked ducts (thick mucus). A person taking enzyme capsules with a meal. A comparison of normal stool vs. steatorrhea (pale, greasy, foul-smelling).
image.caption: Cystic fibrosis can damage the pancreas, preventing digestive enzymes from reaching the intestine. Enzyme replacement therapy (PERT) restores digestion.

classifications.value: Understanding how fats are broken down and why they take a different route into the body.
characteristics: Sources: Oils, butter, fatty meats, nuts, avocados.
Emulsification: Bile salts (from liver, stored in gallbladder) break fat into tiny droplets (emulsification). This increases surface area for enzymes. Without bile, fats are poorly digested.
Enzymes: Pancreatic lipase breaks triglycerides into fatty acids and monoglycerides. Lipase works at the water-fat interface.
Micelles: Fatty acids and monoglycerides combine with bile salts to form micelles—tiny spheres that transport fat to the intestinal cell surface.

Absorption: Fatty acids and monoglycerides diffuse into intestinal cells. Inside cells, they are reassembled into triglycerides. These are packaged with proteins into chylomicrons (large lipoprotein particles). Chylomicrons exit cells and enter lacteals (lymph vessels), not blood.
Route: Chylomicrons travel through lymph → thoracic duct → enters bloodstream near the heart (left subclavian vein). This bypasses the liver initially.
Essential fatty acids: Linoleic acid and alpha-linolenic acid cannot be made by the body and must come from diet.
classifications.behavior:
Fat digestion is different from carbs and proteins in several ways. First, fats don't mix with water—they need bile to emulsify them. Bile salts act like dish soap, breaking large fat droplets into tiny ones that float in water.

Second, fats are absorbed into lymph, not blood. This is because chylomicrons (the particles that carry fat) are too large to enter blood capillaries directly. The lymphatic system can handle these larger particles.

This lymph route also means fats (and fat-soluble vitamins) bypass the liver initially. The liver will eventually process them, but they get a "first pass" through the body first.

The type of fat matters:
• Short-chain fatty acids (from dairy, fermented foods) can be absorbed directly into blood.
• Medium-chain triglycerides (MCTs) are absorbed more quickly and are used in special medical formulas for people with fat malabsorption.
• Long-chain fatty acids (most dietary fats) require chylomicron formation and lymph transport.

Clinical connection: In celiac disease, damage to villi reduces the surface area for absorption. Fats are often malabsorbed because the tips of villi (where fat absorption occurs) are damaged first. Symptoms: steatorrhea (pale, greasy, foul-smelling stools that float), weight loss, deficiency of fat-soluble vitamins (A, D, E, K).

examples.explanation:
Steatorrhea is the presence of excess fat in stool. It's a sign of fat malabsorption: (1) What it looks like: Stools are pale, bulky, greasy, foul-smelling, and often float (because of the fat content). They're hard to flush. (2) Causes: Lack of bile (liver disease, bile duct obstruction), lack of pancreatic lipase (cystic fibrosis, chronic pancreatitis), damage to the small intestine lining (celiac disease, Crohn's disease), or after intestinal surgery. (3) Consequences: Weight loss (fat calories lost), deficiency of fat-soluble vitamins (A, D, E, K), which can cause night blindness (vitamin A), bone disease (vitamin D), bleeding problems (vitamin K). (4) Diagnosis: Fecal fat test (collect stool for 72 hours, measure fat content). Normal <7 g/day; steatorrhea >7 g/day. (5) Treatment: Address the underlying cause. For pancreatic insufficiency, enzyme replacement. For celiac disease, gluten-free diet. For bile duct blockage, surgery or stent.

Why this matters: Steatorrhea is a clue that something is wrong with the digestive system—bile, pancreas, or intestinal lining—and needs investigation.

image.alt: Two toilet bowls: one with normal brown stool (sinks), one with pale, greasy stool (floats). A list of causes: pancreatic disease, bile duct blockage, celiac disease. A diagram showing the consequences (weight loss, vitamin deficiencies).
image.caption: Steatorrhea (fatty stool) is a sign of fat malabsorption. Causes include problems with bile, pancreatic enzymes, or the intestinal lining.

principles.statement: Digestive enzymes are highly specific. Each enzyme breaks only one type of chemical bond. Amylase breaks starch bonds, proteases break protein bonds, lipase breaks fat bonds. This specificity ensures that nutrients are properly processed without breaking down the body's own tissues (except when things go wrong).
principles.application:
This explains why:
• You need multiple enzymes to digest a meal (not one "universal" enzyme).
• Lactose intolerance happens because one specific enzyme (lactase) is missing.
• Pancreatic enzyme replacement includes multiple enzymes (lipase, protease, amylase).
• Digestive enzymes don't digest your own intestines (they're secreted in inactive forms or protected).

Everyday example: Think of enzymes as specialized workers. One worker cuts wood, one cuts metal, one cuts plastic. If you need to break down a chair (wood, metal, fabric), you need all three workers. If the metal cutter is missing, the chair stays intact.
principles.example: In lactose intolerance, the enzyme lactase (which breaks lactose into glucose and galactose) is deficient. Lactose stays intact. It reaches the large intestine, where bacteria ferment it. The fermentation produces gas (H₂, CO₂, methane) and short-chain fatty acids. The undigested sugar also draws water into the colon (osmotic diarrhea). Symptoms: bloating, cramping, flatulence, diarrhea 30 min to 2 hours after dairy consumption. Treatment: avoid dairy or use lactase supplements.
principles.analogy: A key (enzyme) fits only one lock (substrate). Lactase fits lactose, not sucrose. Sucrase fits sucrose, not lactose. That's why you need different keys for different sugars.

image.alt: A diagram showing lock-and-key: amylase fits starch, protease fits protein, lipase fits fat. Lactose intolerance: lactose doesn't fit missing lactase, so it passes undigested to the colon.
image.caption: Digestive enzymes are specific. Lactase breaks lactose; without it, lactose isn't digested and causes symptoms.

principles.statement: The small intestine's massive surface area (300 m²) is essential for absorbing enough nutrients. Without this enormous surface area, you couldn't extract enough calories from food to survive. Conditions that damage the surface (celiac disease, radiation, infection) cause malabsorption.
principles.application:
This explains why:
• The small intestine is so long (20 feet).
• Villi are essential—they're not just decoration.
• Celiac disease (flattened villi) causes severe malabsorption.
• After intestinal surgery (removing part of the small intestine), the remaining parts can adapt and grow more villi, but there are limits.

Everyday example: A flat towel absorbs some water. A terrycloth towel (with loops) absorbs much more because of the extra surface area. Your small intestine is like terrycloth—the villi and microvilli are the loops that grab nutrients.
principles.example: In celiac disease, gluten triggers an immune response that attacks the villi. The villi become flattened and shortened. Surface area drops dramatically. Nutrients can't be absorbed efficiently, leading to: weight loss (can't absorb calories), anemia (iron deficiency), osteoporosis (calcium and vitamin D deficiency), and other deficiencies. The only treatment is a strict gluten-free diet, which allows the villi to regrow (usually over months to years).
principles.analogy: A sponge with many tiny holes soaks up more water than a smooth sponge. Your small intestine's villi and microvilli are like those tiny holes—they create enormous surface area for soaking up nutrients.

image.alt: Normal villi (long, finger-like) vs. celiac disease (flattened, short). A comparison of surface area (large vs. small). A person with weight loss and a gluten-free food list.
image.caption: Celiac disease damages villi, reducing surface area and causing malabsorption. A gluten-free diet allows villi to heal.

principles.statement: Water absorption in the digestive tract is driven by solute absorption. When sodium, glucose, and amino acids are absorbed, water follows by osmosis. This is why oral rehydration therapy works, and why unabsorbed solutes (like lactose) cause diarrhea.
principles.application:
This explains why:
• ORS (sugar + salt) treats diarrhea—it pulls water into the body.
• Lactose intolerance causes diarrhea—unabsorbed lactose draws water into the colon.
• High-sugar drinks can cause diarrhea (if sugar concentration is too high).
• The large intestine absorbs water to form solid stool—failure causes diarrhea.

Everyday example: If you put a raisin in water, it swells because water moves into it (the raisin has higher solute concentration). If you put a raisin in syrup, it shrinks because water moves out. Your intestines work the same way—water moves toward areas of higher solute concentration.
principles.example: In osmotic diarrhea, unabsorbed solutes (like lactose, or the sugar substitute sorbitol) remain in the intestinal lumen. They create a high osmotic pressure, drawing water into the intestine. The result is watery diarrhea. Common causes: lactose intolerance, fructose malabsorption, sugar alcohols (sorbitol, xylitol in sugar-free candy), and magnesium-containing laxatives (magnesium is poorly absorbed and draws water). Treatment: identify and avoid the offending substance.
principles.analogy: Water follows salt. If you have salty water on one side of a membrane and fresh water on the other, water moves to the salty side. In your gut, unabsorbed sugar or salt act like that "salty" side, pulling water in and causing diarrhea.

image.alt: A diagram showing the small intestine: normal (solutes absorbed, water follows) vs. osmotic diarrhea (unabsorbed solutes in lumen, water drawn in). A person with diarrhea and a list of causes (lactose, sorbitol, magnesium).
image.caption: Osmotic diarrhea: Unabsorbed solutes (like lactose) draw water into the intestine, causing watery diarrhea.

components.definition: Carbohydrates → monosaccharides (glucose, fructose, galactose). Proteins → amino acids. Fats → fatty acids, monoglycerides. Transporters: SGLT1 (glucose), GLUT5 (fructose), PEPT1 (peptides), various amino acid transporters. Structures: villi, microvilli, lacteals, blood capillaries.
components.equation:
Surface area of small intestine ≈ 300 m² (tennis court).
Transit time: 2-6 hours for nutrients to be absorbed after a meal.
Absorption efficiency: ~95% of nutrients under normal conditions.
components.interpretation: The huge surface area allows near-complete absorption. Transit time is long enough for nutrients to be broken down and absorbed. When absorption is less than 90%, malabsorption is clinically significant (weight loss, deficiencies).

image.alt: A summary table: Carbohydrates → glucose (blood), Proteins → amino acids (blood), Fats → fatty acids (lymph). A diagram of a villus showing blood capillaries (sugars, amino acids) and lacteal (fats).
image.caption: Summary of nutrient absorption: Carbohydrates and proteins become water-soluble molecules that enter blood; fats become lipid-soluble and enter lymph.

categories.description: Vitamins and minerals are essential nutrients needed in small amounts. They have specific absorption mechanisms, some of which are shared (fat-soluble vitamins with fats, water-soluble vitamins with other transporters).
categories.detailed_mechanism:
Fat-soluble vitamins (A, D, E, K): Absorbed along with dietary fats. Require bile and pancreatic lipase. Malabsorption of fats (celiac, pancreatic insufficiency) leads to deficiency of these vitamins.

Water-soluble vitamins (B complex, C): Absorbed by various transporters. Vitamin B12 is unique—it requires intrinsic factor from stomach. Without intrinsic factor (pernicious anemia, gastric bypass), B12 isn't absorbed, leading to anemia and nerve damage.

Iron: Absorbed in duodenum. Heme iron (from meat) is absorbed better than non-heme iron (plants). Vitamin C enhances absorption; tannins (tea), calcium, and phytates (grains) reduce absorption. Iron deficiency is the most common nutritional deficiency worldwide.

Calcium: Absorbed in duodenum and jejunum. Requires vitamin D. Absorption is regulated by parathyroid hormone and calcitonin. High oxalate (spinach, rhubarb) and phytate (grains) reduce calcium absorption.

Magnesium, zinc, etc.: Absorbed by specific transporters; can compete with each other (high zinc can reduce copper absorption).

examples.explanation:
Vitamin B12 (cobalamin) has the most complex absorption pathway: (1) In the stomach: B12 is released from food by stomach acid. It binds to haptocorrin (R-protein) from saliva. (2) In the duodenum: Pancreatic enzymes break down haptocorrin, releasing B12. (3) Intrinsic factor: Parietal cells in the stomach produce intrinsic factor (IF). IF binds to B12. (4) In the ileum: The IF-B12 complex binds to receptors on ileal cells and is absorbed. (5) Without IF: B12 can't be absorbed. Causes: pernicious anemia (autoimmune destruction of parietal cells), gastric bypass surgery, chronic gastritis. (6) Consequences: B12 deficiency causes megaloblastic anemia (large, immature red blood cells) and neurological damage (numbness, tingling, memory problems, balance issues). (7) Treatment: B12 injections (bypasses the need for IF) or high-dose oral B12 (some is absorbed passively).

Why this matters: B12 absorption shows how multiple organs (stomach, pancreas, small intestine) must work together to absorb a single nutrient—and what happens when one part fails.

image.alt: A diagram of B12 absorption: stomach (intrinsic factor), duodenum (B12 released), ileum (IF-B12 complex absorbed). A person with pernicious anemia getting a B12 injection. A red blood cell comparison: normal vs. megaloblastic (large).
image.caption: Vitamin B12 requires intrinsic factor from the stomach to be absorbed. Without it (pernicious anemia), B12 deficiency causes anemia and nerve damage.

categories.description: The small and large intestines absorb large amounts of water and electrolytes daily. The small intestine absorbs about 8-9 liters per day (from oral intake and digestive secretions). The large intestine absorbs the remaining 1-2 liters, leaving about 100-200 mL in stool.
categories.detailed_mechanism:
Water: Absorbed passively, following solutes (sodium, glucose, amino acids). About 90% absorbed in small intestine, 9% in large intestine, 1% excreted.

Sodium (Na⁺): Absorbed by several mechanisms: SGLT1 (co-transport with glucose), NHE (sodium-hydrogen exchange), and ENaC (epithelial sodium channel, especially in colon).

Potassium (K⁺): Absorbed passively or secreted in colon.

Chloride (Cl⁻): Absorbed passively or via exchange with bicarbonate.

Bicarbonate (HCO₃⁻): Secreted in duodenum (neutralizes acid); absorbed in ileum and colon.

examples.explanation:
Cholera is caused by Vibrio cholerae, which produces a toxin that locks the CFTR chloride channel in the "on" position: (1) Normal: Chloride is absorbed, and water follows. (2) In cholera: The toxin causes massive chloride secretion into the intestine. Water follows, leading to profuse watery diarrhea (up to 20 L/day). (3) Danger: Rapid dehydration can kill within hours. (4) Treatment: Oral rehydration solution (ORS) replaces lost water and electrolytes. The SGLT1 transporter continues to work even during cholera, pulling sodium, glucose, and water into the body. (5) Impact: ORS has reduced cholera mortality from 50% to <1% with proper use.

Why this matters: Cholera shows how disrupting normal electrolyte transport can cause catastrophic fluid loss—and how understanding that transport led to a simple, life-saving treatment.

image.alt: A diagram showing the cholera toxin locking the chloride channel open, causing water to flow into the intestine. A person with severe dehydration (sunken eyes, tenting skin). A person drinking ORS. A graph showing mortality drop with ORS.
image.caption: Cholera toxin causes massive water loss. Oral rehydration solution (ORS) saves lives by using the SGLT1 transporter to pull water back into the body.

sections.section_title: Worked Example: Nutrient Absorption Case Analysis

worked_examples.title: Case Study: Weight Loss and Fatty Stools
worked_examples.problem: A 35-year-old woman has lost 20 pounds over 6 months without trying. She reports her stools are pale, greasy, foul-smelling, and float. She also feels tired and has noticed her vision is worse at night. She has no abdominal pain. (1) What is the likely problem? (2) What nutrients are likely malabsorbed? (3) What might cause this? (4) How would you test for the cause?

step_by_step.calculation: Pale, greasy, foul-smelling stools that float = steatorrhea (fat malabsorption). Weight loss indicates significant malabsorption (fat is calorie-dense). Night vision problems suggest vitamin A deficiency (fat-soluble vitamin).

Fat malabsorption means fat-soluble vitamins (A, D, E, K) are also malabsorbed. Night blindness = vitamin A deficiency. She may also have deficiencies in vitamin D (bone pain), vitamin E (nerve issues), vitamin K (easy bruising).

Fat malabsorption can be caused by: (1) Pancreatic insufficiency (not enough lipase—cystic fibrosis, chronic pancreatitis). (2) Bile duct obstruction (not enough bile—gallstones, liver disease). (3) Small intestine damage (celiac disease, Crohn's disease) where villi are damaged. (4) Bacterial overgrowth. No abdominal pain makes acute pancreatitis or gallstones less likely. Celiac disease or pancreatic insufficiency are high on the list.

Testing: (1) Blood tests—tissue transglutaminase (tTG) antibodies for celiac disease; vitamin levels (A, D, E, K, B12). (2) Fecal elastase (low = pancreatic insufficiency). (3) Upper endoscopy with biopsy (if celiac suspected) to check for villous atrophy. (4) CT scan (if pancreas or bile duct issue suspected).

worked_examples.final_answer: (1) Problem: Steatorrhea (fat malabsorption). (2) Malabsorbed nutrients: Fats and fat-soluble vitamins (A, D, E, K). Night blindness suggests vitamin A deficiency. (3) Possible causes: Pancreatic insufficiency, celiac disease, bile duct obstruction, bacterial overgrowth. (4) Testing: Celiac antibody test, fecal elastase, endoscopy with biopsy, imaging.
worked_examples.concept_applied: Understanding that fat-soluble vitamins are absorbed with fats explains why vitamin A deficiency (night blindness) occurs with fat malabsorption. Different causes (pancreas, bile, intestine) guide which tests to order.

sections.section_title: Process Flow: Fat Absorption (Most Complex Pathway)
sections.introduction: Fat absorption is the most complex nutrient absorption pathway. It requires bile, pancreatic enzymes, and special handling to transport insoluble fats into the body.

process_flow.description: Bile salts (from gallbladder) break large fat droplets into tiny droplets (emulsification).
process_flow.mechanism: Bile salts have a water-loving end and a fat-loving end. They surround fat droplets, breaking them into tiny particles (0.5-1 μm). This increases surface area 1000-fold for lipase to work.

image.alt: A large fat droplet being broken into tiny droplets by bile salts. Diagram shows bile salt structure (water-loving head, fat-loving tail) surrounding fat.
image.caption: Step 1: Bile salts emulsify large fat droplets into tiny droplets, greatly increasing surface area for lipase.

process_flow.description: Pancreatic lipase breaks triglycerides into fatty acids and monoglycerides.
process_flow.mechanism: Lipase works at the water-fat interface. It removes two fatty acids from the triglyceride, leaving one fatty acid attached (monoglyceride).

image.alt: Lipase enzyme cutting a triglyceride (three fatty acids attached to glycerol) into two free fatty acids and a monoglyceride (one fatty acid attached).
image.caption: Step 2: Pancreatic lipase breaks triglycerides into fatty acids and monoglycerides.

process_flow.description: Fatty acids, monoglycerides, and bile salts form micelles—tiny spheres that transport fats to the cell surface.
process_flow.mechanism: Micelles are about 5-10 nm in diameter. They keep the fats soluble in water and transport them to the brush border. Without micelles, fats would separate and not reach the cells.
process_flow.result: Fats delivered to intestinal cell surface

image.alt: A micelle: fatty acids and monoglycerides inside, bile salts on the outside. Micelles moving toward the villus surface.
image.caption: Step 3: Fatty acids and monoglycerides combine with bile salts to form micelles, which transport fats to the intestinal cell surface.

process_flow.description: Fatty acids and monoglycerides diffuse into intestinal cells. Inside, they are reassembled into triglycerides and packaged into chylomicrons.
process_flow.mechanism: Fatty acids diffuse across the cell membrane (passive). Inside the cell, the smooth ER reassembles triglycerides. These are combined with cholesterol, phospholipids, and proteins to form chylomicrons.

image.alt: Fatty acids diffusing into an intestinal cell. Inside, the cell reassembles them into triglycerides and packages them into chylomicrons (large lipoprotein particles).
image.caption: Step 4: Inside intestinal cells, fatty acids are reassembled into triglycerides and packaged into chylomicrons.

process_flow.description: Chylomicrons are released from the cell and enter lacteals (lymph vessels), not blood capillaries.
process_flow.mechanism: Chylomicrons are too large to enter blood capillaries. They exit the cell by exocytosis and enter the lacteal (a blind-ending lymph vessel in each villus).

image.alt: Chylomicrons exiting the intestinal cell and entering a lacteal (lymph vessel). A diagram showing the lacteal in the center of the villus.
image.caption: Step 5: Chylomicrons are too large for blood capillaries. They enter lacteals (lymph vessels) and eventually reach the bloodstream via the thoracic duct.

sections.definition: These are more advanced concepts about nutrient absorption—from the molecular mechanisms of transporters to the conditions that affect absorption.

process_types.energy_profile: SGLT1 is a protein in the membrane of intestinal cells that uses the energy stored in the sodium gradient to pull glucose into the cell against its concentration gradient.
process_types.direction: SGLT1 sits in the brush border membrane. It binds both sodium and glucose on the outside. The sodium gradient (high outside, low inside, maintained by the Na⁺/K⁺ ATPase pump) provides the energy to move glucose into the cell. Inside, glucose exits via GLUT2 into the blood.
process_types.function: SGLT1 allows the small intestine to absorb glucose even when blood glucose is already high (against the gradient). This ensures glucose is absorbed completely.
goals: Understand active transport vs. passive diffusion
Learn how sodium gradients power transport

primary_example.explanation:
The sodium-glucose co-transporter isn't just in the intestine—it's also in the kidneys. In the kidneys, SGLT2 (a similar transporter) reabsorbs glucose from urine back into blood. New diabetes medications (SGLT2 inhibitors, like empagliflozin) block this transporter: (1) Mechanism: Blocking SGLT2 prevents glucose reabsorption. Glucose spills into urine (glycosuria). (2) Effect: Lower blood glucose, weight loss (glucose calories lost), and reduced blood pressure (sodium loss also pulls water). (3) Benefits: In addition to glucose control, SGLT2 inhibitors reduce heart failure hospitalizations and slow kidney disease progression—even in people without diabetes. (4) Side effects: Increased risk of urinary tract infections (glucose in urine feeds bacteria) and rare cases of diabetic ketoacidosis.

Why this matters: Understanding the same transporter in different organs (intestine absorbs glucose, kidney reabsorbs it) led to a new class of diabetes drugs with unexpected cardiovascular and kidney benefits.

image.alt: A diagram of the kidney showing SGLT2 reabsorbing glucose. A medication (SGLT2 inhibitor) blocking the transporter, causing glucose to spill into urine. A person with diabetes taking a pill. Heart and kidney icons showing benefits.
image.caption: SGLT2 inhibitors block glucose reabsorption in the kidney, lowering blood glucose and providing cardiovascular and kidney protection.

image.alt: A diagram of SGLT1 in the intestinal cell membrane: sodium and glucose bind, transporter changes shape, sodium and glucose enter the cell. Na⁺/K⁺ ATPase pump maintains sodium gradient.
image.caption: SGLT1 uses the sodium gradient to pull glucose into intestinal cells against its concentration gradient—active transport.

process_types.type: Short Bowel Syndrome: When Absorption Surface Is Lost
process_types.energy_profile: Short bowel syndrome (SBS) occurs when a large portion of the small intestine is surgically removed (due to Crohn's disease, mesenteric ischemia, trauma, or cancer). Patients cannot absorb enough nutrients and may require parenteral nutrition (IV feeding).
process_types.direction: Removal of small intestine reduces absorption surface area. The severity depends on how much is removed and which sections: duodenum (iron, calcium), jejunum (most nutrients), ileum (B12, bile salts). The remaining intestine can adapt over time—the villi grow longer and wider, and transit slows—a process called intestinal adaptation.
process_types.function: To manage severe malabsorption and prevent malnutrition and dehydration.
goals: Understand how loss of surface area affects absorption
Appreciate the role of specialized nutrition (parenteral nutrition)

primary_example.explanation:
After massive small bowel resection, the remaining intestine undergoes adaptation: (1) Adaptation: Villi grow taller and wider; the bowel diameter increases; transit slows. This increases absorption capacity. Adaptation can take 1-2 years. (2) Limitations: Adaptation may not be enough to achieve enteral autonomy (ability to eat without IV support). (3) Teduglutide: A drug that mimics GLP-2 (a hormone that promotes intestinal growth). It stimulates adaptation, increasing villus height and fluid absorption. (4) Results: Some patients with short bowel syndrome can reduce or eliminate the need for IV nutrition (parenteral nutrition). (5) Challenges: Even with adaptation, patients may need lifelong monitoring for deficiencies, and parenteral nutrition carries risks of infection and liver disease.

Why this matters: Short bowel syndrome shows the critical importance of intestinal surface area and how understanding growth factors can help the gut adapt after massive loss.

image.alt: A diagram of the small intestine showing a large section removed (shaded). Comparison of normal villi vs. adapted villi (taller, wider). A person with an IV line (parenteral nutrition). A teduglutide injection pen.
image.caption: Short bowel syndrome: Loss of intestine reduces surface area. The remaining gut can adapt (villi grow), and medications like teduglutide can enhance this adaptation.

image.alt: A timeline: immediate post-op (short, sparse villi), 6 months (villi longer), 2 years (villi taller, wider). A graph showing absorption capacity increasing over time.
image.caption: Intestinal adaptation: The remaining intestine can adapt after surgery, growing longer villi to increase surface area and absorption.

sections.relationship: Nutrient absorption is the culmination of the digestive process. Carbohydrates become monosaccharides, absorbed by specific transporters (SGLT1, GLUT5) into blood. Proteins become amino acids, absorbed by multiple active transport systems into blood. Fats become fatty acids and monoglycerides, emulsified by bile, digested by lipase, and absorbed into lymph via chylomicrons. The small intestine's enormous surface area—achieved through folds, villi, and microvilli—makes efficient absorption possible. Water and electrolytes follow solutes, with the large intestine recovering the final 10-15% of water to form solid stool. Vitamins and minerals have specialized absorption mechanisms; vitamin B12 requires intrinsic factor, iron requires acid and vitamin C, calcium requires vitamin D. When absorption fails—due to enzyme deficiency (lactose intolerance), surface area loss (celiac disease, short bowel syndrome), or secretory disorders (cholera)—malnutrition and dehydration result. Understanding these mechanisms is essential for treating digestive diseases and for designing effective nutritional therapies.

summary:
Nutrient absorption is the process by which digested food molecules cross the intestinal wall into the body. The small intestine is the primary site of absorption, with an enormous surface area (300 m²) created by folds, villi, and microvilli.

Carbohydrate absorption:
• Starches broken by amylase (mouth, pancreas) → disaccharides → brush border enzymes (maltase, sucrase, lactase) → monosaccharides (glucose, fructose, galactose).
• Glucose and galactose absorbed via SGLT1 (active transport, co-transported with sodium). Fructose via GLUT5 (facilitated diffusion).
• Lactose intolerance: lack of lactase → undigested lactose causes gas and diarrhea.

Protein absorption:
• Proteins denatured by stomach acid; pepsin starts breakdown → pancreatic proteases (trypsin, chymotrypsin) → small peptides → brush border peptidases → amino acids.
• Amino acids absorbed via multiple active transport systems (specific for different amino acid types).
• Dipeptides and tripeptides absorbed via PEPT1.

Fat absorption:
• Bile salts emulsify fats into tiny droplets (increases surface area).
• Pancreatic lipase breaks triglycerides into fatty acids and monoglycerides.
• These combine with bile salts to form micelles (soluble transport particles).
• Fatty acids diffuse into intestinal cells → reassembled into triglycerides → packaged into chylomicrons → enter lacteals (lymph vessels) → eventually reach bloodstream.
• Fat-soluble vitamins (A, D, E, K) absorbed with fats; deficiency occurs in fat malabsorption.

Water and electrolytes:
• Water follows solutes by osmosis.
• Sodium absorbed via SGLT1, NHE, ENaC.
• Oral rehydration solution (sugar + salt) uses SGLT1 to pull water into body during diarrhea.
• Cholera toxin causes massive water loss by locking chloride channels open; ORS saves lives.

Vitamins and minerals:
• Vitamin B12 requires intrinsic factor from stomach; deficiency causes pernicious anemia.
• Iron absorption enhanced by vitamin C; reduced by tannins, calcium.
• Calcium requires vitamin D.
• Malabsorption can cause deficiencies despite adequate intake.

Disorders of absorption:
• Celiac disease: Gluten damages villi → surface area loss → malabsorption of all nutrients.
• Pancreatic insufficiency: Lack of enzymes → undigested fats (steatorrhea) and proteins.
• Lactose intolerance: Lack of lactase → undigested lactose causes osmotic diarrhea.
• Short bowel syndrome: Surgical removal of intestine → reduced surface area → may require IV nutrition.

Understanding nutrient absorption—from the specific enzymes that break down food to the transporters that move molecules across cell membranes—is essential for understanding how the body gets fuel from food and what happens when the process fails.
